text.title: Antipatterns when modelling polymorphic output
text.summary: Common ways teams break discriminated unions: dropping the discriminator, putting it last, and over-using unions when branches are nearly identical.

antipattern.reason: Pydantic falls back to smart union mode and the JSON schema emits `anyOf` without a `discriminator` keyword. OpenAI strict mode then has to try every branch; with overlapping fields this either fails to compile or accepts cross-branch data.

section.title: Discriminator placed last in a branch

antipattern.description: Defining a branch as `class SearchAction(BaseModel): query: str; kind: Literal["search"] = "search"` â€” discriminator at the bottom.
antipattern.reason: Order matters in autoregressive generation. With the literal at the end, the model must commit to `query` before it has chosen the branch, which defeats the grammar-locking benefit. Always declare the discriminator first.

rule.statement: The discriminator field must be the first field of every branch model.
rule.rationale: Constrained decoding only locks the schema branch once the discriminator literal is emitted; placing it first means subsequent fields are constrained from the very next token onwards.

section.title: Union when branches are 90 percent identical

antipattern.description: Modelling `EmailMessage` vs `SmsMessage` as a discriminated union when both share recipient, body, sent_at, and only differ by one channel-specific field.
antipattern.reason: Two near-identical branches double the schema size, double the prompt-cache footprint, and force the model to learn two shapes for one concept. Use a single model with a `channel: Literal["email","sms"]` discriminator and channel-specific optional fields instead.

rule.statement: If two branches share more than ~90 percent of their fields, collapse them into one model with a literal `kind` field and channel-specific optional fields, not into a discriminated union.
rule.rationale: The grammar advantage of unions is paid for by schema size; when the branches barely differ, the cost outweighs the benefit and the model picks the union arm at random.

section.reference: Pydantic guidance on when to use discriminated unions.
